coredata.dc:title: Insights of doxorubicin loaded graphene quantum dots: Synthesis, DFT drug interactions, and cytotoxicity
coredata.dc:creator: Sawy, Amany M.
Barhoum, Ahmed
Abdel Gaber, Sara A.
El-Hallouty, Salwa M.
Shousha, Wafaa G.
Maarouf, Ahmed A.
Khalil, Ahmed S.G.
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), graphene oxide nanosheets (GO), single and multiwalled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs, MWCNTs) exhibit different drug loading capacities, release rates, and targeting abilities. This explains the reported discrepancy of their associated therapeutic efficiencies when used as drug carrier systems. In this study, for the first time, two different types of GQDs named GQDs1 and GQDs2 were synthesized, fully characterized, loaded with the chemotherapeutic Doxorubicin (DOX) and compared with other CNMs under the same conditions. The effects of shape (spheres, tubes and sheets), size (30–180 nm), and surface charge (−64.9 to −11.85 mv) of the synthesized CNMs on DOX loading and release efficiency as well as cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells were investigated. Furthermore, the biosafety of the synthesized GQDs was studied both at the in vitro level using human WI-38 cells and at the in vivo level at low and high doses of 5 and 20 mg/Kg using healthy female Wister rats. Results revealed that GO nanosheets showed the highest DOX loading capacity reaching 2.85 mg/mg while GQDs1 exhibited the highest release rate of 78.1%. The in vitro cytotoxicity evaluation indicated that the smallest spherical nanomaterial among the tested CNMs, namely GQDs1 was the most efficient one on delivering DOX into the cells and inhibiting their proliferation. Regarding the biosafety, all CNMs displayed no noticeable cytotoxicity against WI-38, except for GQDs2. Moreover, hematological, biochemical and histological assessment of both kidneys and livers of treated rats assured the high biosafety level. We also present new insights on the first principle calculations investigating the adsorption of DOX on GO and GQDs. The calculations showed that DOX molecules adsorbed almost equally on both nanoforms, however, the flaky nature of our GO monolayers allowed for sandwich-like structures to exist making its loading capacity superior over GQDs. Based on this comprehensive study, GQDs is the most promising type of the tested CNMs to be used in further studies.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), graphene oxide nanosheets (GO), single and multiwalled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs, MWCNTs) exhibit different drug loading capacities, release rates, and targeting abilities. This explains the reported discrepancy of their associated therapeutic efficiencies when used as drug carrier systems. In this study, for the first time, two different types of GQDs named GQDs1 and GQDs2 were synthesized, fully characterized, loaded with the chemotherapeutic Doxorubicin (DOX) and compared with other CNMs under the same conditions. The effects of shape (spheres, tubes and sheets), size (30–180 nm), and surface charge (−64.9 to −11.85 mv) of the synthesized CNMs on DOX loading and release efficiency as well as cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells were investigated. Furthermore, the biosafety of the synthesized GQDs was studied both at the in vitro level using human WI-38 cells and at the in vivo level at low and high doses of 5 and 20 mg/Kg using healthy female Wister rats. Results revealed that GO nanosheets showed the highest DOX loading capacity reaching 2.85 mg/mg while GQDs1 exhibited the highest release rate of 78.1%. The in vitro cytotoxicity evaluation indicated that the smallest spherical nanomaterial among the tested CNMs, namely GQDs1 was the most efficient one on delivering DOX into the cells and inhibiting their proliferation. Regarding the biosafety, all CNMs displayed no noticeable cytotoxicity against WI-38, except for GQDs2. Moreover, hematological, biochemical and histological assessment of both kidneys and livers of treated rats assured the high biosafety level. We also present new insights on the first principle calculations investigating the adsorption of DOX on GO and GQDs. The calculations showed that DOX molecules adsorbed almost equally on both nanoforms, however, the flaky nature of our GO monolayers allowed for sandwich-like structures to exist making its loading capacity superior over GQDs. Based on this comprehensive study, GQDs is the most promising type of the tested CNMs to be used in further studies.

ce:section.ce:para: Despite the recent progress in treatment strategies and screening techniques, cancer remains one of the world's most devastating diseases, with an annual worldwide diagnosis rate of over ten million new cases [1,2]. Various techniques including, surgical intervention, radiation, and chemotherapy are clinically applied [3]. Doxorubicin (DOX) is one of the chemotherapeutics that are routinely prescribed to breast, pancreatic, ovarian and colorectal cancer patients and manages to suppress tumor proliferation and prolong patient survival [4]. However, DOX sufferes from lack of selectivity and is explicitly distributed through the body causing major side effects [5]. Therefore, the development of non-toxic drug delivery carriers with good permeability in tumor tissue, high loading capacity, and controlled release of the drug is required [6].

Among the plethora of available nanomaterials that have been utilized in drug delivery, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been recently implemented thanks to their good biocompatibility, stable fluorescence, low cytotoxicity, and large specific surface area [7]. GQDs are zero-dimensional crystalline nanostructures consisting of few layers of graphene fragments, usually less than 20 nm in size [8]. Additionally, other Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs) including graphene oxide nanosheets (GO), single and multiwalled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs, MWCNTs) are also promising materials in this regard. They can be easily coupled with various therapeutic and imaging molecules, thus help resolve many of their pharmacokinetics problems such as insolubility, poor biodistribution and the inability to cross cellular barriers [9–11]. GO is an oxidized form of graphene nanosheets with good water dispersibility and biocompatibility [12]. GO consists of hexagonal rings of carbon atoms, bearing oxygen functional groups on its edges such as carboxylic, hydroxyl and epoxide groups. This functionalization enhances the GO loading capacity to carry different types of drug molecules with controlled drug release [13]. CNTs are composed of a single layer of carbon atoms rolled into a tube, they can be single-walled with a diameter of about 1 nm, or multiwalled consisting of several coaxial nanotubes, with diameters reaching more than 100 nm [14]. CNMs difference in geometric orientation and structural composition leading to variations in their loading capacities, release efficiency, intracellular penetration, targeting ability, and a consequent variation in their therapeutic efficiency [15]. Qiu et al. [16] developed a DOX delivery system based on highly fluorescent GQDs with a loading capacity of 0.109 mg/mg and drug release of 60% after 72 h at pH = 5. Wu et al. [17] systematically studied the factors affecting the adsorption properties of DOX onto GO, including reaction time, DOX concentration, pH value, and temperature. GO showed drug loading capacity reaching 1.428 mg/mg at optimum conditions. Comparative studies between CNMs in this regard are scarce and not adequately conducted.

Safety profile of nanoparticles shape the horizon of their use; thus, toxicity assessment is essentially required particularly before using the nanoparticles as drug delivery constructs for therapeutic purposes. Since carbon containing nanoparticles are gaining more interest in this field, toxicity assessment studies are being conducted at different levels. In one of the studies, CdSe-ZnS core-shell QDs were proven to be free of significant toxicity when healthy Sprague-Dawley rats were administered up to 15.0 nmol and for long assessment durations that lasted for up to 80 days [18]. However, on the other hand, in a different setting where the animal model tested was Drosophila, profound toxicities were recorded. The leakage of poisonous Cd2+ ions was believed to be in charge of the observed toxicity in case of CdSe/ZnS QDs when compared to the water soluble InP/ZnS core/shell QDs [19]. Thus, it is difficult to generalize the information about biosafety level of a given nanosystem and further testing is still required.

The nature of drug interaction with the carrier system varies from one carbon family to the other. Generally, DOX can bind effectively with GQDs, GO, and CNTs mainly through the π-π stacking interaction. This binding can be stabilized by electrostatic interaction between negatively charged surface functional groups such as carboxylic and hydroxyl groups and the positively charged amino groups of DOX molecules [17]. On the other hand, H-bond interaction can be involved in the case of GO rich with oxygen functional groups on its surface and DOX may also be encapsulated within the inner space of CNTs [20]. Efforts were exerted to better understand this interaction. Vovusha et al. [21] studied the adsorption of DOX molecules on graphene and GO using density functional theory (DFT) calculations. A systematic investigation of DOX loading onto SWCNTs was carried out by Wang et al. [22]. DOX@CNMs interaction was revealed by fluorescence spectroscopy [23], Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) [24], Zeta potential (ZP) [25], and spectrophotometer (UV–vis) [26]. Better understanding of DOX and CNMs interaction still needs further investigations, since it was found that the surface structure, defects, and functionalization play important roles [17,21]. First-principles-based computational studies of the interaction between DOX and 2D CNMs can provide an understanding and a prediction of their adsorption properties, including loading and release conditions [27].

No previous studies were conducted to compare the five different CNMs, rather in most cases comparisons were limited to only two forms. For instance, Kavosi et al. [28] recently compared the therapeutic efficiency as well as the toxicity of both SWCNTs and MWCNTs, but did not include other CNMs. That has influenced our interest to study different forms of CNMs prepared with different methods, various forms, size and subsequently different properties as DOX delivery system on comparative basis. It is worth mentioning that it is the first study to report using GQDs2 as a drug delivery nanosystem. Parameters of the comparison included DOX loading capacity and release efficiency in a pH-dependent manner, anticancer activity and biocompatibility. We also present new insights on the first principle calculations investigating the adsorption of DOX onto graphene and GO, with configurations inspired by our material characterization ns.

Graphite powder (Sigma Aldrich, ˂20 μm), sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 97%, Piochem), orthophosphoric acid (H3PO4, 85%, Piochem), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 37%, Piochem), potassium permanganate (KMnO4, 99%, Piochem), single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs,˃95%, Nano lab), Dimethylformamide (DMF, 99%, Sigma Aldrich), ammonium molybdate tetrahydrate ((NH4)6Mo7O24· 4H2O, Sigma Aldrich), iron (III) nitrate nonahydrate (98%, Sigma Aldrich), magnesium nitrate (Mg(NO3)2· 6H2O, Sigma Aldrich), and polyethylene glycol (PEG 200, Sigma Aldrich) were used to prepare CNMs.

Doxorubicin hydrochloride (DOX, EIMC United Pharmaceuticals) was used as a drug for cancer treatment. Human adenocarcinoma cell line MCF-7 cells and human normal fibroblast cell line WI-38 were purchased from ATCC. High-glucose Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM), Fetal bovine serum (FBS), penicillin-streptomycin, and 3-(4, 5-Dimethylthiazol −2-yl)-2, 5 diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Biowest. Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 99.9%), Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS, ≥ 99%), Annexin V-FITC Kit, Propidium iodide (PI), RNase, Diamidino-2-phenylindole dihydrochloride (DAPI), Triton X − 100, Glutaraldehyde, Osmic acid, Propylene oxide, Epon 812 resin medium, uranyl acetate, hematoxylin and eosin stain (H&E) and lead citrate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Deionized water was used in all the experiments.

Firstly, GO was prepared according to modified Hummer's method [29] and as previously described in our work [30]. Graphite powder (3 g) was suspended in 400 ml H2SO4/H3PO4 (9:1 V/V). 18 g of KMnO4 was gradually added to the mixture and poured into cold DI water containing 3 ml H2O2 (30%) till a yellow solution was formed. The mixture was washed several times with HCl (10% V/V) and then with DI water until pH = 6 was reached. The obtained black precipitate was dried under vacuum at 60 °C. To obtain the GO nanosheets, the black precipitate was dispersed in H2O and treated with probe sonication at 500 W for 2 h, then filtrated through a 400 nm syringe filter.

Secondly, GQDs1 were prepared from GO nanosheets according to Zhu et al. method with some modifications [31]. 150 mg GO was dissolved in 10 ml DMF (15 mg/ml) and treated with ultrasonication at 400 W for 45 min. The solution was heated at 200 °C for 8 h using a Teflon-lined autoclave (30 ml). Upon cooling to room temperature, the collected mixture was filtrated with a 220 nm syringe filter to eliminate the black precipitates leaving a brown transparent solution, and then the brown precipitate (GQDs1) was obtained after drying under vacuum at 60 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of GQDs (type 2) using chemical vapor deposition (CVD) method
ce:section.ce:para: GQDs2 were prepared using a CVD method through the catalytic decomposition of acetylene in the presence of iron flakes [32]. Fe-Mo-MgO catalyst was prepared following the same previously described steps and transferred into a quartz tube. Firstly, a flow of Ar gas (140 ml/min) at 700 °C was introduced. The acetylene gas passed through the tube at 700 °C. After the reaction, the quartz tube was washed with 200 ml acetone and the greenish-yellow solution was collected after the evaporation of the acetone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and functionalization of SWCNTs and MWCNTs
ce:section.ce:para: MWCNTs were synthesized via CVD method. Fe-Mo-MgO catalyst was prepared according to Logeswari et al. method [33]. Typically, Fe(NO3)3.9H2O, (NH4)6Mo7O24.4H2O, and Mg(NO3)2.6H2O was dissolved in PEG 200 with a molar ratio of 1:1:50 and appropriately distributed in a quartz boat and heated to 550 °C for 10 min under air atmosphere. The yielded foamy material was grounded to a fine powder and calcinated at 800 °C. The as-prepared catalyst was transferred and inserted into the quartz tube under H2 gas flow (0.9 l/min) for 30 min at 700 °C. The acetylene gas was passed through the tube at a flow rate of 0.1 L/min for 30 min at 700 °C. The obtained MWCNTs were calcinated at 450 °C for 1 h to eliminate the amorphous carbon. In order to eliminate the metal catalyst, MWCNTs were refluxed in a mixture of 2:1 V/V (HCL: H2O) for 12 h at 60 °C and washed with DI water.

The commercial SWCNTs were supplied from Nanolab and used as received without further purification. SWCNTs and MWCNTs carboxylation was carried out by acid treatment, in which 0.5 g of CNTs was dispersed in 100 ml H2SO4/HNO3 (1:3 V/V). The CNTs dispersion was treated in an ultrasonic bath for 6 h at 80 °C. The modified CNTs (c-SWCNTs and c-MWCNTs) were collected and dried under vacuum at 60 °C.

CNMs were examined by Transmission electron microscope (TEM, Jeol, 1200EXLL) and Scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS SIGMA 500VP). Raman spectroscopy of CNT was measured using EnwaveProRaman-L with excitation of 532 nm, power of 2 mW, and average scan of 50. The UV–Vis spectra were measured by Carry 60 spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra were recorded using a Spectro-fluorophotometer (AvaSpec-ULS4096CL-EVO). Surface structures were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Jasco6000). The average particle size, as well as the Zeta potential of CNMs dispersions was measured using Zetasizer Nano ZS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DOX loading and release from CNMs
ce:section.ce:para:
DOX loading was carried out by mixing 5 mL of DOX solution (1 mg/ml) with 5 mL of GO, GQDs1, GQDs2, SWCNTs or MWCNTs dispersions (0.25 mg/ml) and stirring at 25 °C for 24 h. The unloaded DOX was removed by ultracentrifugation at 14000 rpm for 1 h until a colorless solution was obtained. The supernatant was collected and the loaded DOX was quantified with UV–Vis spectroscopy at 480 nm using calibration curve of known DOX concentration. The DOX loading capacity of the CNMs was calculated as follows:
(1)
Drug loading capacity =
Wi –
W f
/
W CNMs
where Wi is the initial drug weight; Wf is the final weight of the drug in solution (supernatant) after being loaded, and WCNMs is the weight of the used CNMs.

DOX release was studied at two pH values, pH = 7.4, which mimics the physiological pH as well as the cell culture solution pH and pH = 5 which corresponds to the endosomal pH of tumor cell. A definite amount of DOX loaded CNMs was dispersed in 10 ml Phosphate buffer saline (PBS) or acetate buffer solution with continuous stirring at 37 °C which mimics the body temperature. At each time interval, the dispersion was centrifuged for 30 min for complete precipitation of DOX loaded CNMs leaving the released one suspended in the supernatant. After that, the supernatant was collected and the concentration of the released DOX was quantitatively measured using a calibration curve of different DOX concentrations using UV–Vis spectrophotometer at 480 nm. The DOX release efficiency was calculated using the following formula:
(2)
DOX
release efficiency
%
= amount of released
DOX / amount of loaded
DOX ∗ 100

We performed our computations using the density functional theory plane waves package, Quantum Espresso (QE) [34]. For the exchange-correlation functional, we used the generalized gradient approximation (Perdew-Burke-Ernzerhof), with ultra-soft pseudopotentials. A 6 × 6 GQDs and GO supercells were constructed, with a z-axis separation of 27 Å. This allowed us to have a vacuum separation of at least 17 Å, thus eliminating interaction among neighboring cells. Structural relaxation was performed for all systems, with an atomic force threshold of 10−4 Ry/Bohr. The self-consistent calculations were performed at the Gamma point with an energy cut-off of 45 Ry. The adsorption energies were calculated according to:
(3)
Ead = E
sheet
+ E
DOX
− E
DOX @ sheet
where E(sheet), E(DOX), and E(DOX@sheet) are the total energies of the isolated GQDs/GO sheet, the isolated DOX molecule, and combined DOX@GQDs/GO systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and cytotoxicity efficiency assessment
ce:section.ce:para: MCF-7 and WI-38 cells were cultured separately in DMEM supplied with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin under standard conditions of cell culture (5% CO2, 37 °C). The culture medium was replaced every 2–3 days. All CNMs and DOX@CNMs were filter-sterilized before use. MCF-7 cells were seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 1 × 104 cell / well and incubated till 80% confluency. Unloaded GO, GQDs1, GQDs2, c-SWCNTs, and c-MWCNTs were added at a final concentration series of 3.125 till 200 μg/ml. DOX loaded CNMs and free DOX were added to maintain a final DOX concentration series of 0.39 till 12.5 μg/ml. Cells were incubated for either 24 or 48 h. WI- 38 cells were seeded at a density of 1 × 104 cell / well and incubated till 80% confluency. Unloaded CNMs were added at the same concentration used above. MTT assay was performed to assess cell viability following Chen et al. protocol [23]. The supernatant medium was discarded, 40 μl of MTT solution was added to each well and incubated for 4 h. 150 μl of SDS was added to dissolve the blue formazan crystals. The absorbance of the wells was measured at 570 nm using microplate reader (Bio-Teck 800TS). Cells incubated without nanocarriers were used as control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake of DOX conjugated CNMs and morphological study
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the cellular uptake of DOX@CNMs using confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM), cells were seeded onto coverslips (22 × 22 mm) at a density of 5 × 105 cells/ml and incubated for 24 h. Cells were incubated with either 2.5 μg/ml free DOX or equivalent DOX@CNMs for 2 or 24 h. Cells were then washed three times with PBS and fixed with 3.7% paraformaldehyde for 10 min. The fixed cells were washed three times with PBS and permeabilized with 0.1% triton X-100 for 5 min followed by cells washing with PBS for three times. Then the cells nuclei were stained with 1 μg/ml DAPI for 5 min and washed three times with PBS before examination with CLSM (ZEISS, LSM 710).

In order to investigate the ultrastructural changes of the treated MCF-7 cell, the cells at a density of 1 × 106 cell/ml were seeded in 6-well plates. After 48 h of treatment using the above mentioned DOX concentration, cells were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde for 4 h and then post-fixed in 1% osmic acid for 2 h. The cells pellet was then dehydrated within ascending serious of ethanol starting from 40% till 100%. Cells were placed into propylene oxide and embedded in Epon 812 resin medium. Ultrathin sections with a thickness of ca. 70 nm were cut with an ultramicrotome and mounted on copper grids. The grids were stained with uranyl acetate and lead citrate before being examined with TEM (Jeol, 1200EXLL).

MCF-7 cells were seeded at a density of 1 × 106 cell/ml in 6-well plates and incubated for 24 h. The seeded cells were treated with different CNMs; GQDs1, GQDs2, and c-SWCNTs as well as DOX@CNMs for 48 h. Untreated cells were used as control. The cells were fixed with cooled 70% ethanol for 30 min and then stained with 40 μg/mL propidium iodide (PI) and 100 μg/mL RNase in PBS (pH = 7.4). Finally, the stained cells were analyzed by flow cytometer (Cytomic™ FC500).

The in vivo experimental protocols and procedures used in this study were approved by the animal ethics committee, Kafrelsheikh University. Twenty-five healthy female albino Wistar rats (Rattus norvegicus; weight, 100 to 150 g, age 4–6 weeks) were purchased from the National Research Center (Dokki, Giza, Egypt). In accordance with international guidelines regarding the care and use of laboratory animals, rats were maintained under a 12:12-h light:dark cycle at 22 to 25 °C in a well-ventilated animal facility with a relative humidity of 45.0 (±15)% (Department of Pharmacology, Faculty of Pharmacy, Kafrelsheikh University). Throughout the experimental periods, rats had ad libitum access to drinking water and standard pellet commercial diet.

At the end of the acclimatization period, rats were randomly divided into 5 groups each consisted of 5 rats housed in static polycarbonate plastic cages. The first group was the control group in which rats were administered saline. According to the in vitro studies, the most prominent nanoforms were GQDs1 and GQDs2 and that is why they were the ones selected to be evaluated for their potential in vivo toxicity. For each nanoform, two treatment regimens were applied; low dose (5 mg/kg) and high dose (20 mg/kg). Intraperitoneal injection on daily bases for a total of 14 days was applied to all rats in the 5 groups (amounting to a total of 70 mg /kg in the low dose groups and 280 mg/kg in the high dose groups). Rats were monitored throughout the whole experimental periods for any signs of toxicity or abnormal behavior. At the end of the 2 weeks, animals were weighed, retro-orbital blood collection was conducted, and rats were euthanized by cervical dislocation. Whole blood was used for a complete hematological assessment (white blood cells (WBC), red blood cells (RBC), hematocrit (HCT), mean corpuscular volume (MCV), hemoglobin (HGB), platelets (PLT), mean corpuscular hemoglobin (MCH), and mean corpuscular hemoglobin concentration (MCHC)). Serum biochemical assessments such as levels of total protein (TP), creatinine (CRE), albumin (ALP), glutamic-pyruvic transaminase (SGPT) and glutamic-oxaloacetic transaminase (SGOT) were also analyzed using commercially available kits. Collected liver and kidneys were weighed and specimens from them were fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde for 48 h and then dehydrated and processed for histology. Paraffin blocks of the samples were prepared and sectioned using a rotary microtome into 6 μm thick sections followed by hematoxylin and eosin staining (H&E). The stained slides were examined by light microscopy through a high magnification 400× for a histopathological assessment of possible organ toxicity.

All experiments were carried out at least in triplicate and three independent experiments were repeated. Statistical analysis was performed by Graphpad Instat software using ANOVA and Tukey's Post Hoc test setting the confidence interval as 95% and considering the difference statistically significant when Pvalue <0.05. For P value annotation, the asterisks were used as follows: * for P value ≤0.05, ** for P value ≤0.01, *** for P value ≤0.001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface and morphological analysis of CNMs
ce:section.ce:para: The surface and morphological characterization of the prepared CNMs were carried out. Fig. 1a-d shows the TEM images of the different CNMs. Monodispersed GQDs1 prepared by the solvothermal reduction of GO is shown in (Fig. 1a). The TEM image (Fig. 1b) of GQDs2 synthesized by CVD, shows flakes of stacked graphene nanosheets. Different TEM images with different scale ranges were analyzed to determine the average diameter of GQDs1 and GQDs2. The average particle size of GQDs1 was ca. 5.4 ± 1.3 nm, while GQDs2 showed an average particle size of 16.1 ± 5.2 nm (Fig. S1). GQDs2 looked large in size due to the high aggregations which is a common characteristic of the CVD synthesis with high yield [32]. These results are in good agreement with previous studies reported that GQDs can be prepared with sizes ranging from 3 to 20 nm [35]. GO exhibited a high density of wrinkles (Fig. 1c) due to the introduction of hydroxyl and epoxy functional groups during the oxidation process [17]. MWCNTs are shown in (Fig. 1d). The rope-like tubular carbon structures with the hollow core can be clearly seen. HRTEM of MWCNTs revealed the well-graphitized concentric carbon shells from which the tubes were formed [36]. The SEM and TEM images of smooth surfaced SWCNTs without impurities and with characteristics of 1–5 μm length, 1.5 nm diameter are shown in Fig. S2.

Raman spectroscopy was used as a diagnostic tool to get more information about the structure of the prepared CNMs. As observed in Fig. 1e, different CNMs showed the common characteristic bands of graphene, D and G bands. The D-band and G-band were attributed to the disordered carbon structure and the -C=C- bond in the graphitic plane respectively [37]. GQDs1 showed D and G bands at1372 and 1591 cm−1 respectively with large ID/IG intensity ratio of 0.68. While, in the case of GQDs2, D and G bands at 1370 and 1596 cm−1 respectively were observed with ID/IG ratio of 0.73. The lower ID/IG ratio of GQDs1 reveals the superior crystalline and pure structure of GQDs1 compared to GQDs2. The D and G bands of GO appeared at 1351 and 1602 cm−1 respectively with ID/IG ratio of 0.84. The Raman spectra of pristine SWCNTs (p-SWCNTs) and pristine MWCTNs (p-MWCNTs) showed the D and G bands at 1335 and 1570 cm−1 respectively. Since pristine CNTs are hardly dispersed in aqueous solution, surface functionalization was carried out using acid mixture to create oxygen functional groups on their surface. SWCNTs and MWCNTs surface carboxylation was investigated by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. S3). After carboxylation, the ID/IG intensity ratio increased from 0.54 to 0.95 and from 0.13 to 0.34 in the case of c-MWCNTs and c-SWCNTs, respectively. The increment in the ID/IG ratio is mainly due to the introduction of defects as well as shortening the tubes during the acid treatment [38]. Fig. S4 shows the optical images of water dispersed CNTs before and after carboxylation. It is clearly seen that the p-SWCNTs and p-MWCNTs completely precipitated in water within one hour after dispersion, with the aid of sonication. However, the carboxyl- terminated CNTs (c-SWCNTs and c-MWCNTs) showed better dispersion in water for several days. These results revealed the successful grafting of oxygen functional groups on the CNTs surface and hence, enhancing the dispersion stability of CNTs in water.

The surface charge of different CNMs was determined by Zeta potential measurements. Fig. 1f demonstrates that GQDs1, GQDs2, GO, c-SWCNTs, and c-MWCNTs bear a negatively charged surface over a wide pH range. At pH = 7.4, GO, c-MWCNTs, c-SWCNTs, GQDs1, and GQDs2 beard a surface potential of −64.9, −37.8, −32.3, −27.5, and − 19.1 mV, respectively. At pH = 5, the Zeta potential of all CNMs was also negative, but lower than those obtained at higher pH. These results are in agreement with various reports [17,23]. The elevated negative surface potential of GO, c-SWCNTs, and c-MWCNTs over GQDs suggest more oxygen functional groups on their surface resulting from the oxidation and carboxylation process [39]. The highly negatively charged CNMs overcame the Wan der Waals attraction forces leading to well-dispersed aqueous solutions [40]. Furthermore, the negatively charged surface of CNMs could reduce the plasma protein adsorption and nonspecific cellular uptake [41]. These results confirm that CNMs are favorable candidates for biological applications.
The surface and optical properties of DOX conjugation with the prepared 0-2D CNMs in comparison with free DOX were characterized using UV–Vis, FTIR, PL, and DLS.

Fig. S5 shows the FTIR spectra of CNMs before and after DOX loading. As shown, the free DOX exhibited a characteristic peak at 3436 cm−1, due to NH stretching vibration of the primary amine. The peaks appeared at 2925, 1639 and 1406 cm−1 correspond to quinone, ketone, and carbonyl groups of DOX. While the band at 1546 cm−1 is due to NH stretching vibration [42]. However, in case of DOX loaded CNMs (Fig. S5a-e), the peaks associated with -OH and NH stretching vibrations in DOX were overlapped, broadened and shifted to the lower wavenumber at 3431, 3427, 3313, 3416, and 3419 cm−1 for DOX@GQDs1, DOX@GQDs2, DOX@GO, DOX@c-SWCNTs, and DOX@c-MWCNTs respectively. The characteristic peaks corresponding to quinone and ketone carbonyl groups of DOX were shifted to lower bands 2922, 1636, 1402 cm−1, and 2921, 1635, 1383 cm−1 in the case of DOX@GQDs1 and DOX@GQDs2 respectively, and shifted to 2900, 1637, and 1397 cm−1 in the case of DOX@GO. While for DOX@c-SWCNTs and DOX@c-MWCNTs, the bands appeared at 2923, 1633, 1398 cm−1 and 2924, 1626, 1393 cm−1 respectively. These results demonstrate that DOX was effectively loaded on the different optimized CNMs surface [43,44].

The UV–Vis spectra of the prepared CNMs are shown in Fig. 2a. GQDs1 demonstrated a characteristic peak at 345 nm, while GQDs2 showed multi absorption peaks at 363, 408, 436 nm. These data revealed the multichromophoric character of the as-prepared GQDs2 [45]. GO exhibited a sharp peak at 227 nm and a small shoulder at 300 nm, due to CC and CO groups respectively [46]. Both c-MWCNTs and c-SWCNTs showed a characteristic peak at 260 nm, confirming the existence of -COOH groups on the CNTs surface [47].

Physical and chemical adsorption of DOX onto the prepared CNMs was explicit from UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 2b). When DOX was loaded onto GQDs1 and GQDs2, the absorbance peaks of DOX at 232 nm and 480 nm were red-shifted to 237 nm and 495 nm, and 233 nm and 502 nm, respectively. In the case of DOX@GO, the absorbance peaks of DOX appeared at 234 nm and 498 nm. In the case of DOX conjugated with the c-SWCNTs and c-MWCNTs, the DOX peaks shifted to 235 nm and 499 nm, and 235 and 484 nm respectively. These shifts are mainly due to the ground-state electron donor-acceptor interaction between the DOX and the CNMs indicating that the DOX was successfully loaded onto CNMs [43,48].

The Photoluminescence (PL) spectra originated from DOX@GQDs1 and DOX@GQDs2 are demonstrated in Fig. 2c,d. When GQDs1 exited at 450 nm, it exhibited a strong single peak at 527 nm [16]. While GQD2 exhibited multi emission bands at 477, 507, and 546 nm. This multichromophoric nature may be due to the functional terminal groups at the edge and surface of GQDs2 [45,49]. The free DOX solution demonstrated a peak at 594 nm. The DOX loaded samples, both give two overlapped fluorescence peaks around 586/647 nm and 589/642 nm respectively. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity of DOX showed a significant quenching in the case of DOX loaded systems due to the π-π stacking interaction between DOX and GQDs1 and GQDs2 [23,50].

For further investigations of the stability of the prepared nanocarriers, the average particle size of GQDs1, GQDs2, GO, c-SWCNTs and c-MWCNTs in PBS solution (pH = 7.4), before and after DOX loading as well as the corresponding polydispersity index (PDI) related to the stability of the nanocarriers were measured by DLS at the concentration of 0.1 mg/ml (Table 1 , Fig. S6). After DOX conjugation with CNMs, the average size significantly increased to 99.81, 103.6, 179.2, 231.4 and 257.63 nm for DOX@GQDs1, DOX@GQDs2, DOX@GO, DOX@c-SWCNTs and DOX@c-MWCNTs respectively. The remarkable increase in the average size of CNMs with great polydispersity after DOX loading revealed the successful loading of DOX onto the CNMs surface and/or inner cavity as well as the good stability of the prepared complex. Moreover, to evaluate the stability of the prepared DOX@CNMs conjugates for extended time, the average particle size and the corresponding PDI were measured after storage time, of 10 day. As can be seen from (Fig. S7) the average size of the different conjugates were slightly increased to 122, 121.4, 205, 237.5, and 279.3 nm for DOX@GQDs1, DOX@GQDs2, DOX@GO, DOX@c-SWCNTs and DOX@c-MWCNTs respectively with slight increase in the corresponding PDI. These results reveal the good stability of the different conjugates for over extended time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH-dependent DOX loading and release from CNMs
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the carrier's efficiency, DOX loading capacity of different CNMs was calculated by measuring the absorbance of the loaded DOX at 480 nm. The drug loading capacity of the nanocarriers was determined at physiological pH = 7.4 and endosomal pH = 5 for tumor cells (Fig. 3a). CNMs showed a distinct difference in drug loading capacity at different pH. The descending order of CNMs as DOX carriers was as follows: GO, GQDs1, c-SWCNTs, GQDs2, and c-MWCNTs at pH = 5 where their loading capacities were 1.82, 0.799, 0.7, 0.49, and 0.18 mg/mg respectively. At pH = 7.4, all tested CNMs showed a higher loading capacity of 2.85, 1.66, 1.6, 1.14, and 0.94 mg/mg and the same order was maintained. These differences in loading capacity may be due to the different morphology and surface charges resulting from the terminal groups on the CNMs surface. The high negative charge of GO enhances the coulombic attraction between GO and the positively charged DOX molecules. Furthermore, the DFT calculation revealed that the DOX molecule could adsorb efficiently on the GO surface as the flaky nature of our GO monolayers allows for sandwich-like structures to exist. This clearly explains why GO has a maximum DOX loading capacity compared to other members of the CNMs family [51]. Although, c-MWCNTs exhibited a more negative charge compared to c-SWCNTs, c-SWCNTs showed higher loading capacity. This is very likely due to the higher surface area of c-SWCNTs compared to c-MWCNTs and thus more adsorption sites were available leading to nearly double the loading capacity [52]. The enhanced loading capacity of GQDs1 over GQDs2 may be attributed to the higher negative charge on GQDs1 surface that could enhance the electrostatic interaction between the negatively charged GQDs1 and the positively charged DOX molecule. Furthermore, the smaller size and hence larger surface area of GQDs1 compared to GQDs2 lead to higher loading capacity. Moreover, the negative potential of CNMs at pH = 7.4 was higher than those at pH = 5 as shown in Fig. 1 f. This could enhance the electrostatic interaction between the CNMs and the positively charged DOX at pH = 7.4. In addition, at high pH values, the -OH dissociates to O, further improves the π-π interaction between the quinone portion of DOX and CNMs, and enhancing the adsorption affinity [53]. For these reasons, the drug loading capacity at pH = 7.4 was higher than at pH = 5. A significant enhancement in DOX loading capacity at the optimum conditions of pH = 7.4, T = 25 °C, DOX concentration = 1 mg/ml and CNMs concentration of 0.25 mg/ml was observed in our results. This is by far much more improvement compared to other published studies. For instance, Yang et al. [54] reported GO with a loading capacity of 2.5 mg/mg at pH = 7.4 and initial DOX concentration of 0.47 mg/ml. GQDs drug delivery system with a loading capacity of 0.101 mg/mg at pH = 7.4 and initial DOX concentration of 1 mg/ml was introduced by Qiu et al. [16]. c-MWCNTs with a loading capacity of 0.131 mg/mg at pH = 7.4 and initial drug conc. of 0.3 mg/ml, were obtained by Chen et al. [39]. This enhancement in our case may be due to the different size, surface charge, degree of functionalization as well as the initial drug concentration used. More importantly, the well-dispersity of the DOX loaded CNMs solutions consider as the key factor for providing an accurate calculation of the drug loading under different conditions (pH and concentration).

The DOX release from CNMs was studied at pH = 5 and pH = 7.4 (Fig. 3b). The results showed that the DOX released from CNMs in a controlled and pH-dependent manner. DOX was released from CNMs at basic pH in a slower manner than in case of acidic pH. In particular, the total releasing amount of DOX from GQDs1, GQDs2, c-SWCNTs, c-MWCNTs, and GO, after 72 h at acidic condition in descending order was 78.1, 71.5, 68.3, 65.5%, and 37.5, respectively, which was much higher than at basic ones. Compared to GQDs and CNTs, GO exhibited the lowest release efficacy. We propose that the stronger DOX adsorption onto GO is the mechanism behind the slowed drug-release rate. The high release of DOX from CNMs at acidic conditions may be due to increased hydrophobicity and solubility of DOX at acidic conditions. Furthermore, at acidic pH, the NH2 groups of DOX interact with H+ in the solution forming NH3+ and hence, the weak interaction between DOX and CNMs favors a steady release [42]. These results revealed the pH-sensitive release behavior of different CNMs which make them potential candidates in drug delivery. Several factors can affect the DOX loading capacity and release effeciency of different CNMs as different shapes (cylindrical, nanosheet, spherical), sizes nearly (30 nm–180 nm), and surface charge (−64.9 to −11.85 mv) are shown in Table S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Theoretical calculations of DOX interaction with CNMs
ce:section.ce:para: We now present our computational studies of the DOX on GQDs using first-principles calculations. GQDs and GO were selected for the computational study as a model for all the studied CNMs in this work. We compute the binding energies of DOX to plain GQDs as compared to GO flake. Due to the flake form of GO, it is highly likely that we have structures where DOX molecules are sandwiched between two GO flakes. Furthermore, and since the GO surface can have hydroxyl and epoxy groups, we consider these as two limiting cases encompassing the real binding properties of DOX to GO.

The DOX molecule is initially placed at about 3.5 Å above the GQDs (Fig. 4 ), and all atoms of the system were allowed to structurally relax. The DOX molecule causes some local buckling of the GQDs small sheets, and it extends over a distance of 3.46 Å in the direction normal to the sheet plane. The average distance between the DOX hydrogen atoms facing the sheet is 1.5 Å, while the distance between terminals of neighboring DOX molecules is 2.28 Å. The computed adsorption energy for the DOX@GQDs system is 1.75 eV.

On the other hand, we base our GO systems on a 6 × 6 graphene supercell. This allows for the packing of the DOX on the GO surface in a polymeric way, compared to the isolated structure previously considered [21]. We consider two GO structures, with the oxygen forming epoxy bridges in the first (structural type 1, GO1), and hydroxyl groups (-OH) in the second (structural type 2, GO2). The GO1 and GO2 layers have 10 epoxy groups and 10 hydroxyl groups, respectively, both evenly spread on the graphene surface. This gives us 4 DOX systems: DOX@GO1, DOX@GO2, and the corresponding sandwich structures where the DOX lies between two GO layers (GO1@DOX@GO1, and GO2@DOX@GO2). We add a fifth system with the DOX on a graphene sheet, representing the case of DOX on GQDs (DOX@ GQDs).

The structurally relaxed structures of DOX@GO1 and DOX@GO2 systems are shown in Fig. 5 (a, b). Both GO sheets suffer more buckling than the graphene sheet, due to their sp3 nature. Fig. 5c shows the regions of the various systems components. The DOX molecule extends over a vertical distance of 3.75 Å and 3.90 Å over the GO1 and GO2 planes, respectively. The average distances between the DOX and the hydroxyl and epoxy groups are 2.72 Å and 2.93 Å, and the corresponding binding energies are 2.07 eV and 1.66 eV, respectively. The distance between terminals of the planar DOX neighbors slightly decreases to 1.85 Å and 1.99 Å, respectively.

The sandwich structures are built with a vertical distance of 27 Å between neighboring cells. The GO bilayer has an initial vertical separation of 12 Å between its layers, with the DOX approximately in the middle. This leaves 15 Å between the top layer of one cell and the bottom layer of its neighbor, minimizing the interaction between them. After relaxation, the GO1 bilayer separation decreases to 9.81 Å, due to the layer binding to the DOX molecule (Fig. 6a). The average distance between the DOX and the hydroxyl groups in the two layers are 2.72 Å and 2.49 Å. For the GO2 bilayer (Fig. 6b), the separation decreases to 9.40 Å, and the average DOX-epoxy group distances are 3.08 Å and 3.00 Å. The DOX molecule is now flatter than with the single-layer structures, with a vertical width of 3.53 Å and 3.81 Å above the GO1 and GO2 planes (Fig. 6c) and DOX-DOX terminals distances of 2.10 Å and 1.95 Å, respectively. The binding energies are 3.72 eV and 3.09 eV, respectively, significantly higher than those of the single-layer GQDs and GO structures.

The π-π interactions of DOX with the GQD is larger than with GO1, which is mainly due to the sp2 nature of the GQDs surface, leading to higher binding energy with GQDs. On the other hand, the double-sided sandwich structure, with its two considered limiting cases, allows for a larger interaction surface area that includes π-π and hydrogen bonding, leading to higher binding energies.

The binding energy of the DOX with GO depends on the specific details of the GO surfaces, including the density of its functional groups. Our GO1 and GO2 structures serve as two limiting cases, with GO1 having more hydrogen bonding sites than GO2, which explains its slightly higher binding energy with DOX.

Changing the density of DOX on GQDs and GO systems (by considering larger GQDs /GO supercells) is not expected to significantly change our findings, as is changing the relative orientation of the DOX molecules in the neighboring cells. The DOX-DOX interactions in the considered systems are mainly at a small region in planar neighboring cells, and hence its contribution to the total binding energy is expected to be insignificant compared to the difference between binding energies of different systems. In addition, our use of identical supercells for all considered single layer and sandwich structures further reduces this effect.

The results of our computations interpret the higher loading of DOX on GO. Whereas the DOX molecule adsorbs almost equally on GQDs and GO, the flaky nature of our GO samples allows for the sandwich structures to exist, thereby increasing their loading capacity, giving them some superiority over GQDs for DOX loading.

The cytotoxic effect of the GQDs1, GQDs2, GO, c-SWCNTs, and c-MWCNTs before and after DOX loading and free DOX was comparatively studied using MTT assay. The cytotoxic activity was studied at different concentrations incubated for either 24 h or 48 h. The MCF-7 cells were used as a model for cancer cells. As shown in Fig. 7 (a, b), the cytotoxic effect of GQDs1, GO, c-SWCNTs, and c-MWCNTs against MCF-7 cells revealed non-significant cell death up to 200 μg/ml. The results showed no observed dose-dependent cytotoxicity, and the cell inhibition slightly enhanced by increasing the incubation time. This indicates that these drug carriers did not exert cytotoxic effects and cell proliferation was not influenced. In the case of GQDs2, a slightly high percentage of cell death about 40% was observed at high concentrations that may be attributed to synthesis impurities. Contrarily, after treatment with DOX@CNMs, a high percentage of cell death was observed in a concentration and incubation time-dependent manner Fig. 7 (c, d). The cells showed lower viability when treated with DOX loaded CNMs compared with free DOX. In particular, the half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) (Fig. S.8a) of DOX@GQDs1, DOX@GQDs2, DOX@c-SWCNTs, DOX@GO, DOX@c-MWCNTs, and free DOX was 2.85, 3.9, 5.06, 5.7, 8.15, and 8.14 μg/ml, respectively after incubation for 24 h. The IC50 values of all treatment groups were compared to the free DOX applying one-way ANOVA test and for the intergroup comparison; Tukey's Post Hoc test was applied. New findings from our comparative study have been reached. Particularly, all treatment groups showed a statistically significant enhancement in the activity of DOX when compared to its free form except for when DOX@c-MWCNTs was used. For intergroup comparison, the differences between each following treatment pair (DOX@GO & DOX@GQDs2), (DOX@GQDs2 & DOX@c-SWCNTs), (DOX@GQDs1& DOX@GQDs2) and (DOX@GO& DOX@c-SWCNTs) was not statistically significant and the difference between all other treatment group pairs was statistically significant. It is worth mentioning that the difference between (DOX@GQDs1& DOX@c-MWCNTs) and (DOX@GQDs2& DOX@c-MWCNTs) was highly significant with a P value <0.001. When the incubation time was further increased to 48 h, the IC50 (Fig. S. 8b) was significantly decreased to 0.74, 1.07, 1.69, 1.77, 2.26, and 2.8 μg/ml for DOX@GQDs1, DOX@GQDs2, DOX@c-SWCNTs, DOX@GO, DOX@c-MWCNTs, and free DOX, respectively. IC50 values of different CNMs, free DOX and DOX conjugated CNMs at two different incubation times, 24 h and 48 h are summarized in Table 2 . Comparing the different treatment groups to the free DOX revealed that they all managed to increase its potency seen by the statistically significant lower IC50 values except DOX@c-MWCNTs where there was no significant difference between it and the free DOX. As for the intergroups comparison, the following treatment pairs were not significantly different than each other: (DOX@GO& DOX@GQDs2), (DOX@c-SWCNTs & DOX@GQDs2), (DOX@c-SWCNTs& DOX@c-MWCNTs), (DOX@c-MWCNTs& DOX@GO), (DOX@GQDs1& DOX@GQDs2) and (DOX@GO& DOX@c-SWCNTs). All comparisons between other treatment group pairs showed statistically significant differences. From these results we found that, doubling the incubation period was associated with a decrease in the concentrations needed from all treatment groups including free DOX to reach IC50 condition. That was however associated also with a loss of differential effectiveness between some treatment pairs namely (DOX@c-SWCNTs& DOX@c-MWCNTs) and (DOX@c-MWCNTs& DOX@GO) that showed a statistically significant differences in performance at 24 h of incubation only. Among the treatment groups at both tested incubation periods, using c-MWCNTs as a delivery system did not provide an additive therapeutic value in comparison to free DOX. A different finding might be expected at the in vivo level based on enhanced permeability and retention feature of cancer cells that might be in charge of selective accumulation of the c-MWCNTs loaded with DOX in tumor mass better than free DOX and thus a better treatment outcome. Combining the results of both 24 and 48 h and at the applied test conditions, the same treatment group pairs (eg. DOX@GO&DOX@SWCNTs) that were equally effective at 24 h were found to be equally effective at 48 h. Furthermore, the comparison showed that the difference between DOX@GQDs2 & DOX@GQDs1 was not statistically significant either at 24 h or 48 h incubation times, which maybe due to their similarity in the DOX release efficiency. This may lead to the conclusion that those nanoforms either DOX@GO&DOX@SWCNTs or DOX@GQDs2& DOX@GQDs1 might be used interchangeably and the preference criteria can be rather related to other differential parameters such as easiness of preparation and freedom of inert toxicities. On the other hand, the cytotoxicity caused by DOX@GQDs complex was more efficient than DOX@GO and DOX@CNT, likely owing to the larger size of GO and CNTs to cross the nuclear pores leading to decreasing the drug transportation efficiency to the nucleus [55]. Overall, the results showed that MCF-7 cells toxicity was enhanced after DOX conjugation with CNMs, due to the enhanced loading of the drug onto the CNMs and the efficient internalization mediated by endocytosis.

The drug loading efficiency and IC50 of different carbon-based nanocarriers in comparison with our results are shown in (Table 3 ). The comparison showed great differences in the loading efficiency and IC50 values of the different nanocarriers. These great differences are likely owing to the different sizes, surface charge, surface functionalization, and cell type. On the other hand, the comparison demonstrates a significant improvement in the loading capacity and cytotoxic efficiency in our results.
Fig. S9 shows optical images of MCF-7 cells after treatment with DOX@CNMs for 24 h. As it can be seen, MCF-7 cells were rounded and fell into pieces after incubation with DOX@CNMs for 24 h.

The biocompatibility of CNMs was confirmed by studying their cytotoxicity on normal human lung fibroblast cell lines (WI-38) with MTT assay at different incubation times (24 h and 48 h) and different concentration (Fig. 8a, b). The results demonstrated that CNMs showed no noticeable cytotoxicity against fibroblast cells WI-38 up to 200 μg/ml for GO, GQDs1, c-SWCNTs, and c-MWCNTs independently of the incubation time, and up to nearly 50 μg/ml for GQDs2. The slightly higher toxic effect shown by GQDs2 may be attributed to the presence of some impurities and toxic residuals of the CVD process [45]. In general, the results revealed that CNMs in particularly GO, GQDs1, SWCNTs, and MWCNTs have no cytotoxicity effects and can be safely used as efficient drug delivery agents.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cellular uptake of DOX conjugated CNMs and morphological study
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the cellular uptake and localization of the free form of DOX compared to its equivalent in the DOX@CNMs conjugate using MCF-7 cells over extended time periods, confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) was used. The fluorescence intensity of the as prepared CNMs was relatively weak and the fluorescence emission wavelength of GQDs could overlap with the cellular autofluorescence. Moreover, DAPI and GQDs are both excited at the same wavelength 405 nm [16,64]. Therefore, only the red fluorescence of DOX was tracked. The whole set of excitation conditions including DAPI concentration (1 μg/ml), objective (Plan-Apochromat 63×/1.40 Oil DIC M27), scan mode (plane), lasers intensity (488 nm: 20.0%, 405 nm: 2.0%) and duration were fixed and results were corrected to the control. MCF-7 cells were incubated with free DOX or DOX@CNMs at an equivalent DOX concentration of 2.5 μg/ml for 2 and 24 h. As shown in (Fig. 9 ), after treatment with free DOX and DOX @ CNMs for 2 h, a light purple fluorescence was observed in the nuclear region of MCF-7 cells indicating that the free DOX, as well as the DOX@CNMs, diffused gradually into the nuclei of the cells. When the incubation time was increased to 24 h, a more intense purple fluorescence was observed in the nuclear region of the MCF-7 cells as shown in the merged images in (Fig. 9). It is worth mentioning that no fluorescence was observed in the cytoplasm after treatment for 2 h which revealed that DOX and different CNMs could enter the nuclei of the cells as conjugate and then DOX was released only inside the nucleus which is consistent with results in the literature [64]. However, that was inconsistent with Liang et al. who demonstrated that DOX was first released and then entered the nucleus [62]. This inconsistency in results may be due to the differences in the size, surface properties of the nanocarrier as well as the cell type and subsequently the endocytosis pathway [65,66].

The nuclear DOX mean fluorescence intensity was quantified. As illustrated in (Fig. S10), DOX fluorescence intensity in MCF-7 cells treated with free DOX was 79.5 a.u which was significantly lower than when MCF-7 cells were treated with, DOX@GQDs1 (122.6 a.u), DOX@GQDs2 (104.1 a.u), DOX@GO (97.5 a.u), and DOX@c-SWCNTs (93.6 a.u), respectively. The rapid nuclear uptake and stronger fluorescence intensity in the nuclei of cells treated with DOX@GQDs1, DOX@GQDs2, DOX@GO, and DOX@c-SWCNTs, respectively compared to cells treated with free DOX reveal that the rate and amount of DOX cellular uptake and localization within the cell nuclei could be efficiently enhanced after conjugation with small size CNMs, GO, GQDs1, GQDs2, and c-SWCNTs. However, in the case of large size c-MWCNTs, lower DOX fluorescence intensity (11.04 a.u) was observed. Stronger nuclear DOX fluorescence intensity was observed at longer incubation time 24 h but with the same trend as in the case of 2 h incubation time. It is interesting to note that these results are in good agreement with the cytotoxicity efficiency study results and IC50 values. Both confirmed that most of our prepared small size CNMs; GQDs1 and GQDs2, GO, and SWCNTs respectively could enhance the DOX cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells as compared to free DOX as well as the cellular internalization and accumulation of DOX into the cell nuclei, but with different extent. All these findings reveal the difference in the endocytosis of these different CNMs. CNMs internalization into cells dominantly occurred through endocytosis, which is categorized into four subgroups, caveolae-mediated endocytosis, clathrin-mediated endocytosis, macropinocytosis, and phagocytosis [15]. For instance, small GO can primarily enter the cells through clathrin-mediated endocytosis, but GO with larger size can enter the cells by phagocytosis uptake [67]. Cui et al. [68] reported that macropinocytosis was the major mechanism responsible for the internalization of SWCNTs with length less than 1 μm and without biomolecules coating into RAW264.7 cells. However, many other factors rather than size and shape can affect the mechanism of CNMs internalization such as the surface chemistry, functionalization and stiffness [69].

For further investigation of the ultrastructural changes of MCF-7 cells after treatment with DOX loaded CNMs, TEM analysis was carried out to distinguish between necrotic and apoptotic cell death. Fig. 10a demonstrates the normal characteristics of MCF-7 cells including organelles and uniform nucleus, regularly distributed chromatin, and a clear nuclear membrane. The morphological changes of treated MCF-7 cells with free DOX are shown in Fig. 10 (b, c) in which the cells showed characteristic morphological features of apoptosis including nucleus fragmentation, chromatin condensation, intensive cytoplasmic vacuolization, and plasma membrane blabbing [70,71]. Furthermore, the cells showed the nucleus pyknosis and distortion. MCF-7 cells treated with DOX@GQDs1 are shown in Fig. 10 (d, e). The cell showed nucleus shrinkage, cytoplasmic lysis, and vascularization along with chromatin condensation, nuclear fragmentation as apoptotic features. Besides, cell membrane integrity was lost. In the case of cells treated with DOX@GQDs2 Fig. 10 (f, g) nucleus deformation with chromatin condensation was observed as a characteristic feature of apoptosis. Moreover, the cells displayed nucleus a pyknosis and distortion. For the cells treated with DOX@c-SWCNTs Fig. 10 (h, i) irregular and malformed nuclear morphology with highly condensed chromatin (apoptotic features) was observed. It is clear from the TEM analysis that free DOX and DOX loaded CNMs could effectively induce MCF-7 cell death via inducing cell apoptosis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Flow cytometric analysis of treated MCF-7 cells
ce:section.ce:para: Cell cycle analysis was performed in GQDs1, GQDs2, c-SWCNTs, DOX@GQDs1, DOX@GQDs2, DOX@c-SWCNTs, and free DOX treated cells using PI staining (Fig. 11 ). The untreated cells showed 60.05, 20.01, and 19.94% in the G0/G1, S, and G2/M phases respectively. MCF-7 cells treated with pristine CNMs showed non noticeable changes in cells population percentage in G0/G1, S or G2/M phases as compared to control cells (Fig. 11i). These results are in harmony with the cytotoxicity results; both confirm pristine CNMs have no significant effect on MCF-7 cells proliferation. On the other hand, significant increase in G0/G1 phase population was found in MCF-7 cells treated with free DOX and DOX@CNMs. DOX@GQDs1, DOX@GQDs2, DOX@c-SWCNTs, and free DOX showed 80.3, 70.7, 78.9, and 82.8% G0/G1 phase population, respectively. These results indicated that DOX loaded CNMs, and free DOX had significantly altered cell cycle profile and induced G0/G1 cell cycle arrest. These results are consistent with the arrest in G1 of A549 lung cancer cells following the treatment of DOX [72]. However, other reports showed that DOX induces arrest in G2/M [73]. From the results we can observe that different DOX@CNMs could induce MCF-7 cell apoptosis and cell cycle arrest, which suggest that cell cycle and apoptosis pathways are linked. These findings are in good agreement with Furuya et al. [74] who demonstrated that when both human and rats bearing prostate cancer treated with thapsigargin, Ca2+ release from endoplasmic reticulum was observed leading to depletion of Ca2+ from endoplasmic reticulum and influx of extracellular Ca2+ into the cell. Cessation of cell-cycle progression occurred, and cells were arrested in G0, and further morphological changes associated with apoptosis were visible. Moreover, they reported that apoptosis can occur at any phase of the cell cycle.

One of the major hindering obstacles that prevent the widespread of nanoparticles especially in the biomedical applications is the lack of detailed information about their safety levels [75]. Thus, great attention is given to the evaluation of the toxicity that nanoparticles may exert. Among the set of prepared nanoforms, GQDs1 and GQDs2 loaded with DOX exhibited the most profound anticancer activity at the in vitro level using MCF-7 cells as a model for breast cancer. To assess the possible intrinsic toxicity of those nanoforms at the in vivo level, healthy female Wister rats were used as a preparatory stage for the intended upcoming assessment of their anticancer activity of breast cancer at the in vivo level. Rats received daily intraperitoneal injections of either 5 mg/kg or 20 mg/kg of each nanoform for a total of 2 weeks. No death was reported among the treated groups and no behavioral alterations such as decreased food intake, salivation, motor incoordination, looping or weaving were observed. That was confirmed by the lack of significant weight loss among the treatment groups in comparison to the control group as show in (Fig. 12 a-c). Furthermore, all the tested hematological parameters (summarized in Table.S2) did not show statistically significant alterations in response to the treatment doses of both nanoforms. To further assess the biosafety profile of both nanoforms, serum biochemical assessment of liver functions represented by SGPT and SGOT in addition to kidney function markers such as CRE, ALB and TP level were analyzed as shown in (Fig. 12 d-h). Results assured the biosafety of both GQDs1 and GQDs2 at both tested concentrations as indicated by the lack of statistically significant difference in the treated groups in comparison to the control.

Histological evaluation of H&E stained liver tissues (Fig. 13 ) showed normal hepatic cords radially arranged around central veins (CV) with normal sinusoids (arrows) in all treatment groups. Similarly, H&E stained renal sections (Fig. 13) showed normal glomeruli (G), tubules (T) and interstitial tissues in all treatment groups. These results confirmed the freedom of those nanoforms from any signs of toxicity at both tested doses. Those findings are in accordance with [76,77] that were however conducted on mice rather than rats and revealed the biosafety nature of GQDs in contrast to QDs alone. Interestingly, GQDs did not alter the transcriptome of CD34+ primary human hematopoietic stem cells after 36 h of incubation [78]. It is worth mentioning that although GQDs2 showed profound toxicity at the in vitro level, they were not associated with side effects at the in vivo level. This can be explained by the role played by metabolism and excretion at the in vivo level that is not adequately present at the in vitro one. It is expected that toxicities can be manifested at longer assessment duration and using higher concentrations. However, since we used those nanomaterial as carrier system and keeping in consideration their elevated DOX loading efficiency, 1.6 mg/mg thus 20 mg of GQDs2 would be equivalent to 32 mg of DOX which is much higher than that reported in the literature to be effective [79]. Thus, this concentration was set as the maximum one to be tested in our study. DOX was used at widely varied dosages in previous studies. For instance DOX was reportedly used at dosages varying from 3 to 10 mg/kg [79,80]. In addition Aston et al. [81] was reported that the maximum tolerated dose (MTD) of doxorubicin is 7.5 mg/kg. Therefore, DOX loaded GQDs2 conjugates will be used at equivalent exceptionally low concentration of GQDs2, 4.6 mg/Kg and we can set our safely tested concentration of 20 mg/kg as the maximum one to be tested in our study.

Model organism used in the assessment, tested concentration, dosing regimen, treatment durations and administration routes can account for the discrepancy of the findings regarding the biosafety of the investigated materials. For instance, when zebrafish embryos were used for the toxicity assessment of GQDs, results indicated delays in hatching time and malformation of the embryos at a concentration gradient manner [82,83]. Thus, since we plan future use of rats, they were the selected model in our case for the toxicity assessment and the multiple dosing regimen was selected to simulate the clinical settings and allow for nanoparticle accumulation rather than single dosing.

Nanoparticle toxicity can be related to their surface structure, shape, and size. Surface functional groups and charge can greatly alter the aggregation tendency, biodistribution and thus toxicity profile of the nanoparticles. For instance, it was concluded that pegylation of CdSe/ZnS core-shell (QDs) lowered their associated toxicity profile compared to unpegylated ones. Moreover, the positively charged ones were more toxic than the negative ones [84]. The sizes of the synthesized GQDs1 and GQDs2 were 30.14 ± 3.7 and 52.7 ± 4.3 respectively according to DLS measurements. Being similar to each other can explain the similar biosafety profile they exerted. The pharmacokinetic profile of nanoparticles characterized with low excretion rates and high accumulation levels in vital organs justifies the associated narrow biosafety profile. In case of GQDs, the previous biodistributions studies confirmed their rapid excretion by the kidney and preferential accumulation in tumor tissues [76,85].

Different types of GQDs; (GQDs1, and GQDs2) were synthesized, and tested as drug delivery carriers for breast cancer treatment in comparison with other carbon nanomaterials including GO, SWCNTs and MWCNTs under the same conditions. The results indicated that CNMs with different properties, size, shape, and surface charge showed great differences in their loading capacity, release efficiency, and therefore their therapeutic efficiency. GO with the highest surface negative charge, −67 mV exhibited the highest loading capacity of 2.85 mg/mg at pH = 7.4. GQDs1 and GQDs2 with the lowest surface negative charge, −19.4 and − 11.05 mV showed the highest release efficiency of 78.1 and 71.5% respectively. In vitro studies revealed that the smaller size CNMs, GQDs1 and GQDs2, and GO, SWCNTs could enhance the DOX cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells as compared to free DOX as well as the cellular internalization and accumulation of DOX into the cell nuclei, but with different extent. Contrarily, in the case of large size c-MWCNTs, no additive therapeutic value in comparison to free DOX was observed. In vivo studies including hematological, biochemical, and histological assessment of both kidneys and livers confirmed the freedom of both GQDs1 and GQDs2 nanoforms from any signs of toxicity at both tested doses. Compared to GQDs, DFT calculations revealed that DOX was bound to GO more than graphene in the GO@DOX@GO sandwich configuration. In conclusion, this study shows that discrimination between physiochemical properties of the nanomaterial and their therapeutic efficiencies (regulated mostly by cellular uptake rates) should be taken into consideration when planning biomedical applications. In other words, although GO nanosheets showed the highest DOX loading efficiency, GQDs1 loaded with DOX exhibited the highest release rate and anticancer activity.
